objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: Tropic Trooper
objects.description: A cyber-espionage group that targeted Chinese-speaking individuals in Taiwan, South Korea, and Japan in March 2026. The campaign used a trojanized SumatraPDF reader to deploy the AdaptixC2 Beacon, which leverages GitHub for C2 and VS Code tunnels for persistence.

objects.name: User Execution: Malicious File
objects.description: The victim was socially engineered to execute a trojanized SumatraPDF reader disguised as a military-themed document within a ZIP archive.

objects.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
objects.description: The TOSHIS loader downloaded an encrypted shellcode from a staging server, which was then decrypted and executed in memory to launch the AdaptixC2 Beacon.

objects.name: Web Service: Bidirectional Communication
objects.description: The AdaptixC2 Beacon used the GitHub API for command and control, reading commands from GitHub issues and exfiltrating data by posting to a repository.

objects.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
objects.description: The TOSHIS loader connected to a staging server to download the next-stage encrypted shellcode. Additionally, Microsoft Visual Studio Code was deployed to high-value targets for persistence.

objects.name: Remote Access Software
objects.description: The threat actors used Microsoft Visual Studio Code tunnels to establish persistent, encrypted remote access to high-value targets, blending in with legitimate developer traffic.

objects.name: AdaptixC2 Beacon
objects.description: A custom post-exploitation agent deployed by the TOSHIS loader. It uses the GitHub API for command and control by reading commands from issues and exfiltrating data to a repository.

objects.name: TOSHIS
objects.description: A loader, identified as a variant of Xiangoop, that is executed by the trojanized SumatraPDF. It connects to a staging server to download and execute an encrypted shellcode payload, which in turn launches the AdaptixC2 Beacon.

objects.name: EntryShell
objects.description: A custom backdoor previously associated with Tropic Trooper, found hosted on the campaign's staging server.

objects.name: SumatraPDF
objects.description: A legitimate PDF reader that was trojanized and used as a lure. Upon execution, it displayed a decoy document while deploying the TOSHIS loader in the background.

objects.name: Cobalt Strike
objects.description: A Cobalt Strike Beacon was found hosted on the staging server, indicating its use as part of the attacker's toolset for post-exploitation activities.

objects.name: Microsoft Visual Studio Code
objects.description: The legitimate developer tool was abused by the threat actors to establish persistent, encrypted remote access via VS Code tunnels for high-value targets.

objects.name: Tropic Trooper Staging Server
objects.description: An IP address (158.247.193.100) used to host and deliver payloads, including an encrypted shellcode for the AdaptixC2 beacon, a Cobalt Strike Beacon, and the EntryShell backdoor.

objects.name: GitHub C2 Infrastructure
objects.description: The threat actors used GitHub repositories and its API for command and control of the AdaptixC2 Beacon. Commands were read from GitHub issues and exfiltrated data was posted to the repository.

objects.name: VS Code Tunnel Infrastructure
objects.description: Microsoft Visual Studio Code tunnels were used to provide persistent, encrypted remote access to high-value targets, blending C2 traffic with legitimate developer activity.

objects.name: Comparative Analysis of US-UK and US-Australia Nuclear Submarine Cooperation (2025).exe
Tropic Trooper Staging Server IP
objects.description: Indicator: Tropic Trooper Staging Server IP
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Tropic Trooper Staging Server Connection
id: 9b2d8c3f-1e4a-4b7c-8d5e-6f9a0b1c2d3e
status: experimental
description: Detects outbound network connections to a known Tropic Trooper staging server IP address used to host payloads.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/tropic-trooper-uses-trojanized.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1105
  - attack.command_and_control
level: high
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    DestinationIp: '158.247.193.100'
    Initiated: true
  condition: selection

objects.name: Tropic Trooper Lure Filename Execution
objects.description: Indicator: Tropic Trooper Lure Filename Execution
objects.pattern: [file:name = 'Comparative Analysis of US-UK and US-Australia Nuclear Submarine Cooperation (2025).exe']
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Tropic Trooper Lure File Execution
id: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-4a7b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of a malicious file used as a lure in a Tropic Trooper campaign.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/tropic-trooper-uses-trojanized.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
  - attack.execution
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\Comparative Analysis of US-UK and US-Australia Nuclear Submarine Cooperation (2025).exe'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Tropic Trooper GitHub C2 Pattern
objects.description: Detects network traffic to GitHub API endpoints for issues, which is used by AdaptixC2 for C2. This is a behavioral hint and may have false positives from legitimate developer tools.
objects.pattern: [ipv4-addr:value = '158.247.193.100'] OR [file:name = 'Comparative Analysis of US-UK and US-Australia Nuclear Submarine Cooperation (2025).exe']

objects.name: Tropic Trooper GitHub C2 Pattern
objects.description: Detects network traffic to GitHub API endpoints for issues, which is used by AdaptixC2 for C2. This is a behavioral hint and may have false positives from legitimate developer tools.
objects.pattern:
title: Tropic Trooper C2 Communication via GitHub API
id: f8a1b2c3-d4e5-4f6a-7b8c-9d0e1f2a3b4c
status: experimental
description: Detects network traffic patterns consistent with the AdaptixC2 beacon using GitHub issues for command and control.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/tropic-trooper-uses-trojanized.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1102.002
  - attack.command_and_control
level: medium
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    c-uri|contains|all:
      - 'api.github.com/repos/'
      - '/issues'
  filter:
    c-useragent|contains:
      - 'GitHub'
      - 'git/'
  condition: selection and not filter

objects.name: VS Code Tunnel Command-Line Execution
objects.description: Detects the command-line execution used to initiate a VS Code tunnel, a technique used by Tropic Trooper for persistence.
objects.pattern:
title: Visual Studio Code Tunnel Execution
id: 1a2b3c4d-5e6f-4a7b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of the 'code tunnel' command, which can be used by threat actors like Tropic Trooper for persistent remote access.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/tropic-trooper-uses-trojanized.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1219
  - attack.command_and_control
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\code.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: ' tunnel'
  condition: selection

objects.name: VS Code Tunnel Network Connection
objects.description: Detects DNS queries for the domain used by VS Code tunnels. While potentially legitimate, it can indicate malicious persistence when observed from unexpected systems.
objects.pattern:
title: Visual Studio Code Tunnel Network Connection
id: e5f6a7b8-c9d0-4e1f-8a2b-3c4d5e6f7a8b
status: experimental
description: Detects outbound network connections to the domain used for VS Code tunnels. Connections from servers or non-developer workstations are highly suspect.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/tropic-trooper-uses-trojanized.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1219
  - attack.command_and_control
level: medium
logsource:
  category: dns_query
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    QueryName|endswith: '.rel.tunnels.api.visualstudio.com'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Tropic Trooper APT Targets Chinese Speakers with Trojanized PDF Reader, Uses GitHub for C2
objects.description:
## Executive Summary
The advanced persistent threat (APT) group **[Tropic Trooper](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0081/)** (also known as APT23, Pirate Panda) has been attributed to a new cyber-espionage campaign observed in March 2026. Researchers at **[Zscaler's](https://www.zscaler.com/)** ThreatLabz report with high confidence that the group is targeting Chinese-speaking individuals, likely in Taiwan, South Korea, and Japan, with military-themed lures. The attack chain begins with a trojanized version of the legitimate **SumatraPDF** reader. When executed, it deploys a custom post-exploitation agent called **AdaptixC2 Beacon**. In a novel command-and-control (C2) tactic, this beacon uses **[GitHub](https://github.com/)**'s API to receive commands and exfiltrate data, hiding its traffic within legitimate developer activity. For persistent access to high-value targets, the group was also observed using **[Microsoft Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/)** tunnels. The campaign leverages a complex toolset including a loader named **TOSHIS** and a backdoor called **EntryShell**, reinforcing the attribution to Tropic Trooper.

## Threat Overview
**Threat Actor:** **Tropic Trooper** (APT23, Earth Centaur, KeyBoy, Pirate Panda)
**Targets:** Chinese-speaking individuals and organizations, with a focus on Taiwan, South Korea, and Japan.
**Malware:** **AdaptixC2 Beacon**, **TOSHIS** (loader, variant of Xiangoop), **EntryShell** (backdoor), **[Cobalt Strike](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0154)** Beacon.
**Infrastructure:** `158.247.193.100` (staging server), GitHub repositories (for C2).
**Primary Goal:** Cyber espionage and long-term intelligence gathering.

The campaign demonstrates Tropic Trooper's continued evolution, incorporating living-off-the-land techniques and abusing legitimate services to evade detection. The use of military-themed documents suggests a focus on government, defense, or political targets.

## Technical Analysis
The attack is multi-staged and relies on social engineering to initiate.
1.  **Initial Access & Execution:** ([`T1204.002 - Malicious File`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204/002/)) The victim receives a ZIP archive with a lure document, such as `Comparative Analysis of US-UK and US-Australia Nuclear Submarine Cooperation (2025).exe`. This executable is a trojanized version of SumatraPDF.
2.  **Defense Evasion & Payload Delivery:** ([`T1140 - Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1140/)) Upon execution, the legitimate PDF reader displays a decoy document. In the background, it runs a loader called **TOSHIS**. TOSHIS connects to a staging server (`158.247.193.100`) to download an encrypted shellcode.
3.  **Beacon Deployment:** The shellcode is decrypted and executed in memory, launching the **AdaptixC2 Beacon**.
4.  **Command and Control:** ([`T1102.002 - Bidirectional Communication`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1102/002/)) The AdaptixC2 Beacon uses the GitHub API to communicate. It reads commands from GitHub issues and exfiltrates data by posting to the repository, effectively using GitHub as its C2 server.
5.  **Persistence & Remote Access:** ([`T1105 - Ingress Tool Transfer`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1105/)) For high-value targets, the actors deploy Microsoft Visual Studio Code and establish a **VS Code tunnel**. This provides them with persistent, encrypted remote access that blends in with legitimate remote development or administration traffic.

The staging server was also found hosting a **Cobalt Strike** Beacon and a custom backdoor named **EntryShell**, tools previously associated with Tropic Trooper.

## Impact Assessment
The impact of this campaign is primarily espionage-related. By gaining persistent access to the systems of individuals in government, policy, or defense circles, Tropic Trooper can exfiltrate sensitive documents, monitor communications, and gather long-term intelligence. The use of VS Code tunnels provides a stealthy and resilient foothold within a target network, making eviction difficult. While not directly destructive, the loss of confidential strategic, political, or military information can have significant national security implications for the targeted countries. The abuse of trusted platforms like GitHub and VS Code for malicious purposes also makes detection harder for network defenders, as blocking these services is often not feasible.

## IOCs — Directly from Articles
| Type | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| ip_address_v4 | `158.247.193.100` | Staging server used to host payloads. |
| file_name | `Comparative Analysis of US-UK and US-Australia Nuclear Submarine Cooperation (2025).exe` | Malicious executable masquerading as a document. |

## Cyber Observables — Hunting Hints
Security teams may want to hunt for the following patterns to detect this activity:

| Type | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| url_pattern | `api.github.com/repos/{user}/{repo}/issues` | Network traffic pattern for C2 communication via GitHub issues. Monitor for suspicious or unauthorized processes making these API calls. |
| process_name | `code.exe` | Execution of Visual Studio Code in a server environment or by a user who is not a developer could be suspicious. |
| command_line_pattern | `code tunnel` | The command used to initiate a VS Code tunnel for remote access. Monitor for this in command-line logs. |
| network_traffic_pattern | Outbound connections to `*.rel.tunnels.api.visualstudio.com` | The domain used for VS Code tunnel connections. Connections from servers or non-developer workstations are highly suspect. |

## Detection & Response
- **Network Monitoring**: Monitor for outbound connections to `api.github.com`. While legitimate, traffic from non-developer tools or suspicious processes should be investigated. Use **[URL Analysis](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:URLAnalysis)** to inspect the full API path for suspicious repository names.
- **Endpoint Detection**: Deploy EDR to monitor for process execution chains. A PDF reader spawning network connections to GitHub or launching command shells is highly anomalous. Create detection rules for the `code tunnel` command line.
- **Application Control**: Use application allowlisting ([`D3-EAL: Executable Allowlisting`](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:ExecutableAllowlisting)) to prevent the execution of unauthorized applications like the trojanized `SumatraPDF.exe`.
- **Response**: If a VS Code tunnel is detected, treat it as an active hands-on-keyboard incident. Isolate the host and investigate all activity performed through the tunnel. Revoke any credentials that may have been used or exposed on the compromised machine.

## Mitigation
- **User Training**: Train users to be suspicious of unsolicited attachments, especially executables masquerading as documents ([`M1017 - User Training`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1017/)).
- **Email Security**: Implement email security gateways to scan and block malicious attachments and ZIP files.
- **Restrict Web-Based Content**: If feasible, restrict or monitor outbound connections to code-hosting platforms like GitHub from non-developer systems ([`M1021 - Restrict Web-Based Content`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1021/)).
- **Disable or Remove Feature or Program**: For server environments, disable or remove developer tools like VS Code unless there is a clear business need. This prevents their abuse for persistence ([`M1042 - Disable or Remove Feature or Program`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1042/)).
